feed.title: Marginal Notes
feed.subtitle: A newsletter about reading, writing, and getting by.
feed.rights: Copyright 2026 Jennifer Williams. Poems and prose the author's own.

entry.title: Issue No. 18, the comfort of rereading
entry.summary: I went back to Bluets a third time and stopped pretending it was for any reason other than comfort.
entry.content: I went back to Bluets again, the third time, and I have stopped pretending it is for any reason other than comfort. Maggie Nelson built a whole architecture out of one color and 240 numbered pieces. I have been pairing it with Ross Gay, who runs toward the feeling with his arms open. Issue 7 of Thread and Bone is coming together, theme is inventory. If you are local, I am hosting the Alchemy open mic on Friday the 23rd and will have copies in the tote, five dollars, exact change appreciated.

entry.title: Issue No. 17, the fall reading window
entry.summary: Three poems out to Copper Nickel, and a note on sending work into the quiet.
entry.content: The fall reading windows are open, so a fresh batch went out this month. Three poems to Copper Nickel, two more to Beloit on a suggestion from Professor Wentworth. Submitting is mostly waiting, and the waiting teaches patience whether or not you want the lesson. From the desk, the found poem from a receipt finally holds its shape.

entry.title: Issue No. 16, two of three taken
entry.summary: A small good thing, an acceptance from Poetry Northwest.
entry.content: Some news, quietly. Poetry Northwest took two of the three poems I sent in the fall. It is the kind of yes that does not change the rent but does change the week. Thank you to everyone who read the rough versions and told me the truth about them. The tip jar poem stays home a little longer, it is not done.

entry.title: Issue No. 15, batch cooking as a writing practice
entry.summary: Sunday dal, a full fridge, and the way a small routine protects the writing.
entry.content: Sunday is batch day, red lentil dal and a tray of roasted vegetables, lunches lined up so the weekday mornings belong to the page and not the stove. Structure enables the art, or at least it enables getting to the desk before the shift. This week, a poem about the shared kitchen and who leaves the light on.

entry.title: Issue No. 14, a year in reading
entry.summary: The books that held the year, from Carson to Clifton.
entry.content: The year in books, kept short. Autobiography of Red, which I measure others against. Lucille Clifton, who could say more in eight lines than most of us manage in eighty. Ross Gay for the joy, Nelson for the ache. If a single one of these found you at the right moment, that is the whole point of the letter.
